title: Executive Order 9674—Liquidation of War Agencies

flags.justification: The Director of Liquidation's role is explicitly advisory and coordinative ('further proper preparations,' 'advise and assist') — each war agency develops its own winding-up plans (Sec. 2a); the Director standardizes practice with a seven-agency Advisory Committee providing real input, not operational control.
The Director operates in consultation with the Advisory Committee and receives periodic status reports from each war agency (Sec. 2c) — a structured accountability flow.
Single coherent purpose: coordinating the orderly wind-down of temporary wartime agencies.
An advisory, coordinative function directed at other government agencies, not a binding compliance regime against third parties.
No compliance regime exists for an incentive structure to attach to.
The Director's function is inherently temporary (coordinating the winding-down of other temporary agencies), yet no explicit termination date is set for the Director's own role.
Sec. 3 explicitly states 'nothing in this order shall be deemed to divest any agency... of any of its functions' — protective, preserving existing agency authority.
The seven-agency Advisory Committee provides real, broad-based interagency input into the Director's coordination function.
A significant but carefully bounded coordination mechanism for the largest institutional wind-down in the corpus to this point; the text claims only advisory authority, not broad operational power.
Sec. 3 explicitly protects against exactly this concern: 'nothing in this order shall be deemed to divest any agency... of any of its functions.'
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 1/16 = 6.25%. A well-designed meta-coordination mechanism for winding down the entire wartime agency apparatus, carefully scoped to advice rather than operational control.
